key_insight: The paper turns short-range-order alloy design into a differentiable optimization problem, enabling direct gradient-based search from a target SRO pattern or property to an alloy structure.
engineering_relevance: This offers a faster, scalable route to inverse design of high-entropy alloys, helping engineers tune stiffness and other properties without relying on slow random-swap searches.
method: Treat atom occupancy as continuous so gradient descent can optimize SRO matching directly.
Add a physics-based correction term to keep generated structures thermodynamically realistic across many elements.
Use a small neural network to map composition and SRO statistics to mechanical properties, closing the loop from target property to design.
evidence_signal: Across nine FCC and BCC alloys, the pipeline reproduced SRO-driven stiffness shifts from -20% to +57%, matched 3 of 4 Co-Cr-Ni target stiffness values within 6%, and showed 864+ atoms are needed for reliable trend prediction.
watch_item: Results depend on sufficiently large supercells; commonly used 108-atom cells can give misleading property trends, so cell-size convergence must be checked.
paper_title: Differentiable inverse design of short-range order in high-entropy alloys: from target sro to target property
